subject: Physics
q:
The mean free path of a molecule of diameter $5 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$ at the temperature $41^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and pressure $1.38 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$, is given as $\_\_\_\_$ m.
(Given $k_{B}=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$).
question:
The mean free path of a molecule of diameter $5 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$ at the temperature $41^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and pressure $1.38 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$, is given as $\_\_\_\_$ m.
(Given $k_{B}=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$).
solution:
Mean free path is calculated using $\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P}$
Given: $d = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $T = 41°C = 314$ K, $P = 1.38 \times 10^5$ Pa, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
$\lambda = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 314}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times (5 \times 10^{-10})^2 \times 1.38 \times 10^5}$
$= \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 314}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times 25 \times 10^{-20} \times 1.38 \times 10^5}$
Numerator: $433.32 \times 10^{-23}$
Denominator: $1.414 \times 3.14159 \times 34.5 \times 10^{-15} = 153.18 \times 10^{-15}$
$\lambda = \frac{433.32 \times 10^{-23}}{153.18 \times 10^{-15}} = 2.83 \times 10^{-8}$ m = $2\sqrt{2} \times 10^{-8}$ m

subject: Physics
q: In an ideal gas at temperature T, the average force that a molecule applies on the walls of a closed container depends on <math><mi>T</mi><mi> </mi><mi>a</mi><mi>s</mi><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow></msup></math> . A good estimate for q is:
question: In an ideal gas at temperature T, the average force that a molecule applies on the walls of a closed container depends on <math><mi>T</mi><mi> </mi><mi>a</mi><mi>s</mi><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow></msup></math> . A good estimate for q is:
solution:
Average linear velocity for collision to occur

t=2du

Change in momentum in 1 collision

Δp=2 mu

∴     average force in collision

= Δpt

(u= root mean square speed)

=2 mu2d ×u

⇒    F∝u2

∴     u2 ∝T

⇒    F∝T

⇒   q=1

subject: Physics
q: One kg of a diatomic gas is at a pressure of $8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The density of the gas is $4 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{-3}$. What is the energy of the gas due to its thermal motion?
question: One kg of a diatomic gas is at a pressure of $8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The density of the gas is $4 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{-3}$. What is the energy of the gas due to its thermal motion?
solution:
Thermal energy corresponds to internal energy
Mass $=1 \mathrm{~kg}$
density $=8 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$
$\Rightarrow$ Volume $=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { density }}=\frac{1}{8} \mathrm{~m}^3$
Pressure $=8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$
$\therefore$ Internal Energy $=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{P} \times \mathrm{V}=5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}$

q: On the basis of kinetic theory of gases, the gas exerts pressure because its molecules:
question: On the basis of kinetic theory of gases, the gas exerts pressure because its molecules:
options: continuously stick to the walls of container.
suffer change in momentum when impinge on the walls of container.
continuously lose their energy till it reaches wall.
are attracted by the walls of container.
solution: From the assumption of KTG, the molecules of gas collide with the walls and suffers momentum change which results in force on the wall and hence pressure.

q: If the root mean square velocity of hydrogen molecule at a given temperature and pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the root mean square velocity of oxygen at the same condition in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
question: If the root mean square velocity of hydrogen molecule at a given temperature and pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the root mean square velocity of oxygen at the same condition in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
solution:
Root mean square velocity is given by, Vrms=3RTM

Therefore, V1 V2=M2M1⇒2 V2=322

⇒V2=0.5 km s-1

subject: Physics
q: To raise the temperature of a certain mass of gas by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at a constant pressure,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn></math> calories of heat is required. When the same mass of gas is cooled by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at constant volume,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn></math> calories of heat is released. How many degrees of freedom does each molecule of this gas have (assume gas to be ideal)?
question: To raise the temperature of a certain mass of gas by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at a constant pressure,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn></math> calories of heat is required. When the same mass of gas is cooled by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at constant volume,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn></math> calories of heat is released. How many degrees of freedom does each molecule of this gas have (assume gas to be ideal)?
solution:
At constant pressure :

∆Q=nCP∆T ⇒   160=nCP.50  .....(1)

At constant volume

∆Q=nCv∆T ⇒  240=nCv.100......(2)

Equation (1) divided by (2)

160240=CPCV·50100

CPCV=43=1+2f

f=6

subject: Physics
q:
The rms speed of oxygen molecule in a vessel at particular temperature is 1+5x12v, when v is the average speed of the molecule. The value of x will be:

(take π=227)
question:
The rms speed of oxygen molecule in a vessel at particular temperature is 1+5x12v, when v is the average speed of the molecule. The value of x will be:

(take π=227)
solution:
The RMS speed is given by 

vrms=3RTM

The average speed is given by 

vavg=8RTπM

The ratio is 

⇒vrmsvavg =3RTM8RTπM=3π8=38×227⇒vrmsvavg =3328

Comparing it with the given value 1+5x12,

vrmsvavg =1+528⇒x=28

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: The internal energy of a perfect gas is entirely kinetic and depends only on absolute temperature of the gas and not on its pressure or volume.
Statement 2: A perfect gas is heated keeping pressure constant and later at constant volume. For the same amount of heat the temperature of the gas at constant pressure is lower than that at constant volume.
question:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: The internal energy of a perfect gas is entirely kinetic and depends only on absolute temperature of the gas and not on its pressure or volume.
Statement 2: A perfect gas is heated keeping pressure constant and later at constant volume. For the same amount of heat the temperature of the gas at constant pressure is lower than that at constant volume.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement- 2 is true and Statement $-2$ is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true but Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true.
solution:
Internal energy of a system is the energy possessed by the system due to molecular motion and molecular configuration. Internal energy $u=u_k+u_p$ For an ideal gas there is no molecular attraction $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{p}}=0$
$\therefore$ Internal energy $\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}$ (i.e., internal kinetic energy)
and $\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{3}{2} \mu \mathrm{RT}$
At constant pressure $(\Delta \mathrm{Q})_{\mathrm{P}}=\mu \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}} \Delta \mathrm{T}$
At constant volume $(\Delta \mathrm{Q})_{\mathrm{v}}=\mu \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{T}$
As C $_{\mathrm{P}}>\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}} \quad \therefore \quad(\Delta \mathrm{T})_{\mathrm{P}} < (\Delta \mathrm{T})_{\mathrm{V}}$

subject: Physics
q: The plot that depicts the behavior of the mean free time <math><mi>τ</mi></math> (time between two successive collisions) for the molecules of an ideal gas, as a function of temperature <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> qualitatively, is: (Graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
question: The plot that depicts the behavior of the mean free time <math><mi>τ</mi></math> (time between two successive collisions) for the molecules of an ideal gas, as a function of temperature <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> qualitatively, is: (Graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
solution:
The average time taken by a moving particle between successive collisions or impacts is referred to as the mean free time τ.

The average distance a moving particle travels between successive collisions or impacts is referred to as the mean free path λ.

λ=kT2πd2P

and vrms=3RTM

So, the mean free time τ=λvrms

Therefore, τ∝1T

solution:
The RMS value of a gas is given by 

vrms=3RTM=3kBTm

Substituting the given values,

vrms=3×1.4×10-23×3004.6×10-26
≈523 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: If the collision frequency of hydrogen molecules in a closed chamber at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{Z}$, then the collision frequency of the same system at $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is :
question: If the collision frequency of hydrogen molecules in a closed chamber at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{Z}$, then the collision frequency of the same system at $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is :
solution:
Assuming mean free path constant.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f} \propto \mathrm{V} \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{T}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{f}_1}{\mathrm{f}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}}=\sqrt{\frac{300}{400}} \\ & \mathrm{f}_2=\sqrt{\frac{4}{3}}=\mathrm{f}_1=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{Z}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{nf}_1 \mathrm{RT}}{2} \\& \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{i}}=-78^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \rightarrow 273+\left[-78^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right]=195 \mathrm{~K}
\end{aligned}$
K.E $\alpha \mathrm{T}$
To double the K.E energy temp also become double
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=390 \mathrm{~K} \\
& \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}=117^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

solution:

solution:
As per Equi-partition law,

each degree of freedom contributes
12 kBT average energy.

In mono atomic gas, D.O.F.=3
⇒ Average energy =3×12 kBT=32 kBT

subject: Physics
q: The volume of an ideal gas increases 8 times and temperature becomes $(1 / 4)^{\text {th }}$ of initial temperature during a reversible change. If there is no exchange of heat in this process $(\Delta \mathrm{Q}=0)$ then identify the gas from the following options (Assuming the gases given in the options are ideal gases):
question: The volume of an ideal gas increases 8 times and temperature becomes $(1 / 4)^{\text {th }}$ of initial temperature during a reversible change. If there is no exchange of heat in this process $(\Delta \mathrm{Q}=0)$ then identify the gas from the following options (Assuming the gases given in the options are ideal gases):
solution:
For an adiabatic process ($\Delta Q = 0$) with an ideal gas: $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$
Given: $V_f = 8V_i$ and $T_f = \frac{1}{4}T_i$
Applying the adiabatic relation:
$T_i V_i^{\gamma-1} = T_f V_f^{\gamma-1}$
$T_i V_i^{\gamma-1} = \frac{T_i}{4}(8V_i)^{\gamma-1}$
$1 = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 8^{\gamma-1}$
$4 = 8^{\gamma-1}$
$2^2 = 2^{3(\gamma-1)}$
$2 = 3(\gamma-1)$
$\gamma = \frac{5}{3} = 1.67$
This value corresponds to a monatomic gas. Among the options, only He is monatomic ($\gamma = 5/3$ for monatomic ideal gases). O₂ and N₂ are diatomic ($\gamma = 7/5$), CO₂ is polyatomic ($\gamma \approx 1.30$), and NH₃ is polyatomic.

subject: Physics
q: A gas mixture consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> moles of argon and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> moles of oxygen at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Neglecting all vibrational modes, the total internal energy of the system is
question: A gas mixture consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> moles of argon and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> moles of oxygen at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Neglecting all vibrational modes, the total internal energy of the system is
solution:
As we know, internal energy is given by, U=nCvT. Also argon gas is monoatomic and oxygen gas is diatomic.

Therefore, total internal energy will beUnet=n1CV1T+n2CV2T

=8×3R2×T+6×5R2×T

=27RT

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> moles of a polyatomic gas<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>)</mo></math> must be mixed with two moles of a monoatomic gas so that the mixture behaves as a diatomic gas. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> is:
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> moles of a polyatomic gas<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>)</mo></math> must be mixed with two moles of a monoatomic gas so that the mixture behaves as a diatomic gas. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> is:
solution:
The equivalent degree of freedom feq of the mixture is

feq=n1f1+n2f2n1+n2

For diatomic gas feq=5.

⇒5=(n)(6)+(2)(3)n+2

⇒5n+10=6n+6

⇒n=4

subject: Physics
q: The number of air molecules per cm3 is increased from 3×1019 to 12×1019. The ratio of collision frequency of air molecules before and after the increase in number respectively is :
question: The number of air molecules per cm3 is increased from 3×1019 to 12×1019. The ratio of collision frequency of air molecules before and after the increase in number respectively is :
solution:
The given data is 

n1=3×1019n2=12×1019

The mean time between two collision has the meaning of a period τ, since it indicates the repetitive time intervals in which on average collisions take place. The reciprocal of the time τ can be understood as the collision frequency f, which indicates the number of collisions per unit time.The collision frequency is also directly proportional to the number molecules per unit volume.

f∝1τ

⇒f∝n

⇒f1 f2 =n1n2=3×101912×1019=0.25

subject: Physics
q: A sample contains mixture of helium and oxygen gas. The ratio of root mean square speed of helium and oxygen in the sample, is:
question: A sample contains mixture of helium and oxygen gas. The ratio of root mean square speed of helium and oxygen in the sample, is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{ms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{w}}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{He}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{w}, \mathrm{He}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{w}, \mathrm{O}_2}}} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{4}{32}}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{He}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{1} \\ & \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: 1 mole of a gas with $\gamma=7 / 5$ is mixed with 1 mole of a gas with $\gamma=5 / 3$, then the value of $\gamma$ for the resulting mixture is
question: 1 mole of a gas with $\gamma=7 / 5$ is mixed with 1 mole of a gas with $\gamma=5 / 3$, then the value of $\gamma$ for the resulting mixture is
solution: $\gamma$ for resulting mixture should be in between $7 / 5$ and $5 / 3$

subject: Physics
q: A diatomic gas with rigid molecules does <math><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of work when expanded at constant pressure. What would be the heat energy absorbed by the gas, in this process?
question: A diatomic gas with rigid molecules does <math><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of work when expanded at constant pressure. What would be the heat energy absorbed by the gas, in this process?
solution:
Cp=72R (for diatomic gas)
ΔQ=nCpΔT ∴Isobaric process
ΔW=nRΔT=10 J
Given
∴ΔQ=n×72RΔT=72×10=35 J nRΔT=10 J

subject: Physics
q: What will be the average value of energy along one degree of freedom for an ideal gas in thermal equilibrium at a temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is Boltzmann constant)
question: What will be the average value of energy along one degree of freedom for an ideal gas in thermal equilibrium at a temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is Boltzmann constant)
solution:
Energy associated with each degree of freedom per molecule =12kBT.

Hence, the average value of energy along one degree of freedom for an ideal gas in thermal equilibrium at a temperature T =12kBT.

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> moles of diatomic gas in a cylinder is at a temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Heat is supplied to the cylinder such that the temperature remains constant but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> moles of the diatomic gas get converted into monoatomic gas. The change in the total kinetic energy of the gas is
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> moles of diatomic gas in a cylinder is at a temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Heat is supplied to the cylinder such that the temperature remains constant but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> moles of the diatomic gas get converted into monoatomic gas. The change in the total kinetic energy of the gas is
solution:
Initial kinetic energy of the system,

Ui=N52RT

Final kinetic energy of the system,

Uf=2n32 RT+N-n52RT

⇒Uf=12nRT+52NRT

ΔU=Q=12nRT.

subject: Physics
q: For a gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mtext>P</mtext></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mtext>V</mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> in a state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mtext>P</mtext></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mtext>V</mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>R</mi></math> in a state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mtext>P</mtext></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mtext>Q</mtext></msub></math> are the temperatures in two different states <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, respectively. Then
question: For a gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mtext>P</mtext></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mtext>V</mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> in a state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mtext>P</mtext></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mtext>V</mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>R</mi></math> in a state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mtext>P</mtext></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mtext>Q</mtext></msub></math> are the temperatures in two different states <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, respectively. Then
solution: CP-CV=R for ideal gas and gas behaves as ideal gas at high temperature, so TP>TQ.

subject: Physics
q: Nitrogen gas is at 300 °C temperature. The temperature (in K ) at which the rms speed of a H2 molecule would be equal to the rms speed of a nitrogen molecule, is ………. (Molar mass of N2 gas 28 g ).
question: Nitrogen gas is at 300 °C temperature. The temperature (in K ) at which the rms speed of a H2 molecule would be equal to the rms speed of a nitrogen molecule, is ………. (Molar mass of N2 gas 28 g ).
solution:
Given,

Temperature of Nitrogen gas is 

T1=300 °C=300+273=573 K

Molar mass of H2 gas is MH2=2 g

Molar mass of N2 gas is MN2=28 g

The rms speed of gas is given by

 Vrms=3RTM

Now rms speed of hydrogen gas is equal to rms speed of nitrogen gas, i.e.; VrmsH2=VrmsN2
⇒TH2TN2=MH2MN2

Substitute the given values i above equation, we get,

TH2=228×573

TH2=41 K
